FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Years 2 (2018-2020) and Year 4 (2020-2022), 4,443 participants, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, data release 6.0.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: adjusted linear regression and modified Poisson regression examining associations of screen time and step count with cardiovascular outcomes (blood pressure, cholesterol, HbA1c).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article; no evidence of prior duplicate in corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper (Tables 2, 3, S2-S6). The sole exposure is total recreational screen time as a categorical composite. Step 2: The composite is defined in methods as encompassing 'watching television and videos, playing video games, texting, social media, and video chat' — it bundles TV, video games (non-phone/non-SM), and phone/SM activities with no per-modality breakout anywhere in the paper. Under Step 2 rules this composite fails because it includes TV and video games without a separate phone/SM breakout.

notes: The Screen Time Questionnaire composite mixes TV/video viewing, video games, texting, social media, and video chat into a single total. No sub-score for phone/SM-only is reported in main tables or any supplementary table (S2-S6). Fails IC2 Step 2.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, four waves (Years 1-4), Baseline N=11,878; 21-site U.S. cohort, ages 9-10 at baseline, data release 5.1.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: multi-level modeling and multi-group latent growth curve models examining smartphone ownership age and parent-child internalizing problem discrepancy across 4 waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article; no evidence of prior duplicate in corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Smartphone ownership age (age of first smartphone) is the key phone variable. Table 3 and Table 4 report multi-level model coefficients for smartphone ownership groups (8&under, 9&10, 11&12, 13&14) predicting youth- and parent-reported internalizing problems (anxiety/depression measured via CBCL and BPM). For example, Table 3: Informant effect (beta2) = -0.85, SE=0.08, p<.001 in 8&under group; Table 4 shows b=-1.05, z=-5.04, p<.001 for the 13&14 group at Year 4. Smartphone ownership is a qualifying phone variable; internalizing problems (anxiety/depression) is an individual-trait-level outcome. Individual-trait on the other side confirmed.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multi-level model path coefficients (b, beta, SE, p) for smartphone ownership predicting internalizing problems are reported throughout Tables 3 and 4 and Figure 1. Quantitative results present for the SM/phone <-> individual-trait relationship.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric estimates (b, beta, SE, z, p) reported throughout Tables 3 and 4.

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone ownership age is the IV (grouping variable) throughout; internalizing problems is the DV. Smartphone/phone does not appear as DV in any analysis. EC7 requires SM/phone to be DV in every analysis — here it is IV. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Screen time also appears as a within-person covariate with its own coefficient (Table 3: Screen time -> IP = 0.05, SE=0.01, p<.001 for 8&under group), but the qualifying phone variable is smartphone ownership age as IV predicting an individual-trait (internalizing problems) as DV.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, T1 N=11,787, four annual waves (T1: M_age=9.9 through T4: M_age=12.9), 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: random-intercept crosslagged panel models examining bidirectional associations between electronic media use and family conflict across 4 waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article; no evidence of prior duplicate in corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social EMU is defined in methods (p.1029) as a composite of 'social media + texting' — exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items. Crosslagged path coefficients for social EMU <-> family conflict are reported in Figures 3 and 4 (for boys and girls separately) and in results text. Example: girls social EMU T1 -> family conflict T2: b=-0.59, beta=-0.18, SE=0.02, p<.001. Family conflict (family functioning scale) is an individual-trait-level variable. Qualifying phone/SM variable (social EMU = social media + texting) paired with individual-trait outcome (family functioning/conflict). MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Crosslagged path coefficients (b, beta, SE, p) for social EMU <-> family conflict reported in Figures 3, 4 and results text. Also total EMU coefficients in Figure 1 (e.g., within-person total screen time T->T+1: b=0.04, beta=0.04, SE=0.01, p=0.002).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric estimates (b, beta, SE, p-adj values) reported throughout main figures and results text.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional crosslagged design: social EMU (and total EMU) appears as both IV (predicting later family conflict) and DV (predicted by earlier family conflict) within the same crosslagged panel models. SM/phone is not DV-only. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Social EMU composite (social media + texting) qualifies under Step 2 as exclusively phone/SM items. Video-viewing EMU (TV/YouTube) and video game EMU are also analyzed separately but are not needed for the IC2 determination since social EMU already satisfies Step 1.
